title: Prawn Cocktail
subtitle: Cold cooked prawns in Marie Rose sauce on crisp shredded lettuce with lemon
excerpt: Prawn cocktail is a starter built in three layers: cold shredded lettuce, cold cooked prawns, and a Marie Rose sauce of mayonnaise, ketchup, lemon, and Worcestershire. It was the defining British restaurant starter of the 1970s and has never really gone away.
sourceNotes: British restaurant tradition. Prawn cocktail is documented in British cookbooks from the 1960s and became the most ordered restaurant starter in Britain through the 1970s. The Marie Rose sauce is attributed to Fanny Cradock, who popularised it in the 1960s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the sauce up to 2 days ahead. Dress the prawns and assemble no more than 30 minutes before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A British sauce made from mayonnaise and tomato ketchup, seasoned with lemon juice, Worcestershire sauce, and cayenne. The standard accompaniment for prawn cocktail, and one of the few sauce preparations that carries a specific name in British domestic cookery.

content.text: Prawn cocktail is a cold assembled dish with no cooking. The variables are the quality of the prawns and the balance of the sauce. Marie Rose sauce is mayonnaise and ketchup with lemon, Worcestershire, and cayenne; the ratio of these components determines whether the sauce tastes fresh and sharp or sweet and flat. Make it in advance and taste it cold before assembling.

items.prepNote: iceberg or little gem, outer leaves discarded, shredded
half for the sauce, half in wedges to serve

content.text: : mix the {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{ketchup}} of ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, a squeeze of lemon juice, and a pinch of cayenne. Season with salt and pepper. Taste: it should be tangy and slightly sharp. Adjust the lemon and cayenne to your preference.
Pat the {{prawns-cooked}} of cooked prawns dry with kitchen paper. Reserve a few for the top of each glass. Fold the rest into the sauce.

Divide the shredded {{lettuce}} lettuce between four glasses or bowls. Spoon the prawn mixture over the lettuce. Lay a few reserved prawns on top. Chill for 15 minutes before serving with lemon wedges.

items.cause: Too much ketchup or not enough acid.
items.fix: Add more lemon juice and a little more Worcestershire sauce. Taste before the prawns go in and again after folding them together.

items.symptom: Lettuce is watery at the bottom
items.cause: Lettuce not dried after washing, or assembled too far in advance.
items.fix: Dry shredded lettuce thoroughly in a salad spinner or with kitchen paper. Assemble no more than 30 minutes before serving.

items.cause: Pre-cooked prawns cooked again, or very low-quality prawns.
items.fix: Pre-cooked prawns should not be heated. Use the best quality available; flavourless, watery prawns will make the whole dish flat.

content.text: Prawn cocktail was the most ordered restaurant starter in Britain through the 1970s. Its popularity was such that it became shorthand for a whole era of British restaurant culture, and its fall from fashion in the 1980s and 1990s was equally dramatic. It has since returned as a retro standard and genuine favourite, which is the normal trajectory of any dish whose quality depends entirely on ingredients and balance rather than technique. The prawns must be cold, the lettuce crisp, and the sauce made with a decent mayonnaise.
